text_blocks.text: Feynman devoted the latter half of his book
What Do You Care What Other People Think? to
his experience on the Rogers Commission, straying
from his usual convention of brief, light-hearted
anecdotes to deliver an extended and sober
narrative. Feynman's account reveals a disconnect
between NASA's engineers and executives that was
far more striking than he expected. His interviews
of NASA's high-ranking managers revealed starting
misunderstandings of elementary concepts. For
instance, NASA managers claimed that there was a
1 in 100,000 chance of a catastrophic failure
aboard the shuttle, but Feynman discovered that
NASA's own engineers estimated the chance of a
catastrophe at closer to 1 in 200. He concluded that
the space shuttle reliability estimate by NASA
management was fantastically unrealistic, and he was
particularly angered that NASA used these figures
to recruit Christa McAuliffe into the Teacher-in-Space
program. He warned in his appendix to the
commission's report (which was included only after
he threatened not to sign the report), "For a
successful technology, reality must take precedence
over public relations, for nature cannot be fooled."
